one_line_takeaway: A first-of-its-kind systematic review of 146 studies mapping how six non-functional properties of code LLMs — including security (poisoning/backdoors) and privacy (memorization/membership inference) — are evaluated and enhanced, and where the gaps (stealthy-attack detection, scalable robustness defenses) remain.
key_finding: Per-property main findings (paper's Table 1 and per-section 'Findings' boxes): (Security) LLM4Code is materially exposed to data-poisoning/backdoor attacks because training data is easy to access and alter; research is trending toward stealthier, harder-to-detect triggers, and existing detectors (spectral signature, activation clustering, ONION) are largely ineffective against them — a defense gap. (Privacy) Models leak PII/secrets memorized from open-source training data and effectiveness of mitigations is unproven; membership inference is a double-edged tool (attack and unauthorized-use detection); model stealing and gradient leakage are underexplored. (Robustness) LLM4Code is broadly non-robust; most defenses require expensive retraining and are tested on small models, so scalable defenses for billion-parameter models are lacking. (Efficiency) Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (esp. LoRA) is the dominant efficiency trend; quantization/pruning are comparatively underexplored, and efficiency's impact on robustness/security is unknown. (Explainability) Different explanation techniques disagree; work skews to classification (defect prediction) over generation, and end-user needs are unmet. (Usability) Productivity impact of tools like Copilot is genuinely mixed (positive and negative), studies concentrate on completion/chat rather than security tasks, and few design suggestions are validated in practice.
core_contribution: This is a systematic literature review (SLR), not a new technique or system. Its own contribution is the review itself: the authors position it as the FIRST systematic review of the non-functional properties of large language models for code (LLM4Code) beyond accuracy. From 146 primary studies they (1) define and organize a set of properties beyond accuracy — robustness, security, privacy, explainability, efficiency, and usability (the abstract/intro repeatedly claim 'seven' properties but only enumerate these six); (2) for each property, build a two-part taxonomy of how it is EVALUATED and how it is ENHANCED in the literature (e.g., for robustness, six test-input-generation strategy families — gradient-based, heuristic-driven, search-based, reinforcement-learning, style-transformation, transferability-based, plus 'unspecified', catalogued in Table 3); and (3) synthesize challenges/opportunities into a three-view framework for future LLM4Code development — data-centric, human-centric, and system-centric. IMPORTANT for the knowledge graph: every concrete method, attack, defense, and benchmark discussed in the paper (e.g., stealthy backdoor triggers, spectral-signature detection, 160x model compression, Copilot productivity studies) belongs to CITED third-party works, not to this paper. The authors run no experiments of their own; their empirical output is bibliometric.
threat_model: As a survey the paper does not propose a novel threat model; it catalogs the threat landscape that surveyed works assume, with LLM4Code as the VICTIM. Two security-relevant clusters are surveyed. (1) Security (Section 4.2): training-time data poisoning / backdoor (trojan) attacks — attackers who can modify or inject examples into publicly shared code datasets or open-source repositories (aided by fake GitHub accounts) implant triggers so the model emits attacker-chosen outputs (e.g., vulnerable code, fake API recommendations, wrong summaries) on triggered inputs while behaving normally otherwise; also black-box inference-time attacks (few-shot prompt inversion, prefix-tuning, adversarial NL instructions) that coax deployed Code LLMs into generating vulnerable-but-functional code. (2) Privacy (Section 4.3): training-data memorization leading to extraction of secrets/PII (API keys, passwords, emails, IPs), membership-inference attacks (used both offensively for extraction and defensively to detect unauthorized data use), plus underexplored model-stealing and gradient-leakage. Robustness (Section 4.1) is treated as adjacent to security: semantic-preserving perturbations (variable renaming, style transforms) flip predictions of e.g. vulnerability detectors deployed in CI/CD, letting malicious commits slip through.
limitations: (1) The paper claims 'seven important properties beyond accuracy' throughout, but only six are ever enumerated (robustness, security, privacy, explainability, efficiency, usability) — an unreconciled internal inconsistency. (2) Property counts are inconsistent between prose and figures (e.g., privacy stated as 16 in Section 2.2 prose but 14 in Fig. 1b). (3) Single primary database (DBLP); although justified, this plus keyword-truncation search risks missing relevantly-titled work, and 32.6% of the corpus are non-peer-reviewed arXiv preprints. (4) Fast-moving target — the authors' own footnote warns that conclusions about commercial systems (e.g., ~40% Copilot vulnerability figure) may already be stale. (5) As a survey it contributes no new measurements, benchmarks, or validated defenses; the challenges/opportunities section is speculative. (6) Snowballing depends on Semantic Scholar citation coverage. Note: the extracted 22-page text ends mid-Section 5.2, so the paper's own Section 6 'Threats to Validity' and conclusion were not captured in the ground-truth text used for this review.

datasets_used: Corpus of 146 LLM4Code non-functional-property papers (2019-2024), gathered from DBLP; initial search Dec 2 2023, collection date Feb 20 2024
Semantic Scholar API used for 8 rounds of backward+forward snowballing (added 23 papers)
51 additional non-LLM4Code papers catalogued for comparison (215 papers total across Fig. 2)

Benchmark datasets appear only in Table 4 as artifacts USED BY SURVEYED robustness works (not by this survey): CodeSearchNet, Devign, SySeVR, ReVeal, BigCloneBench, OJClone, HumanEval, MBPP, APPS, Google Code Jam, Py150, Java-small/med/large, CodeXGLUE
quantitative_results: Survey's own bibliometrics: final corpus 146 LLM4Code papers
Per-property distribution (Section 2.2 prose): robustness 27, efficiency 21, usability 20, security 16, privacy 16, explainability 16 (Fig. 1b), 'Other'/reviews 11
Only 2 non-LLM4Code security papers vs 16 LLM4Code security papers, evidencing new security research demand created by LLMs
Venue mix: 32.6% arXiv preprints, ICSE 7.9%, ASE 6%, FSE 3.7%, TOSEM 3.7%, ~9.8% emerging venues
CITED (third-party, not the survey's own experiments): Pearce/Hammond et al. — ~40% of GitHub Copilot programs contained vulnerabilities
CITED: Wan et al. — spectral-signature backdoor detection on CodeBERT achieved recall 6.10%, precision 8.63% (i.e., ineffective)
CITED: Niu et al. — ~8% (43) of crafted prompts leaked privacy info from Copilot; Huang et al. — extracted 2,702 hard-coded credentials from Copilot and 129 secrets from CodeWhisperer
CITED: Shi et al. — compressed CodeBERT/GraphCodeBERT to 3MB (160x smaller), 4.31x/4.15x faster inference, retaining 96.15%/97.74% of vulnerability-prediction performance
CITED: Peng et al. — developers with Copilot completed an HTTP-server task 55.8% faster (95% CI 21-89%)
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines (survey). Positions itself against prior LLM4Code/LLM4SE literature reviews it deems narrower or accuracy-focused:
Hou et al. — SLR of LLMs for software engineering, 229 papers 2017-2023
Fan et al. — survey of LLMs for SE with open problems; Nguyen et al. — research agenda for generative models in SE
She et al. — taxonomy of pitfalls of LLMs in SE; Zheng et al. — survey of code LLMs (evolution/benchmarking)
Zhuo et al. — survey of data augmentation for code models; Hussain et al. — survey of trojan attacks in neural code models; Lo — vision for trustworthy AI for SE
